content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

If you look at the recent headlines regarding the mental health of young Indians, the numbers are nothing short of a systemic failure. Studies are now showing that nearly 70% of Indian students and young professionals suffer from moderate to high anxiety and depression.
When we talk about this crisis, the corporate world often blames “hustle culture,” social media, or a lack of resilience in the younger generation. But having spent two decades deep in the trenches of the Indian IT Service industry, I know the real reason.
The mental health crisis in our young tech professionals isn’t an accident. It is a direct by-product of an industry model where senior leadership has abandoned the codebase to chase profitability, cascading impossible pressure onto the 23-year-olds on the ground.
Here is what is actually happening behind the glowing quarterly revenue reports.
The “Pyramid Model” and the Death of the Technical Senior
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Indian IT Services industry was built on a labor-arbitrage “pyramid model.” To maintain high profit margins, companies require a massive base of low-cost freshers and juniors, managed by a rapidly narrowing peak of senior management.
Because of this structure, a toxic cultural norm has emerged: Writing code is viewed as “blue-collar” IT work.
If you have 10 to 15 years of experience, you are heavily pressured to abandon technical execution. To get a promotion, a higher salary, or respect, you must become a Project Manager, Delivery Manager, or Scrum Master.
The result? The people in charge of software delivery no longer understand modern software development. Their Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) shift entirely away from software architecture, code quality, or system resilience. Instead, they manage by Excel spreadsheet. Their only concerns are resource utilization, billable hours, and account margins.
The Cascade of Unnecessary Pressure
When management is completely detached from the reality of the code, the environment becomes a pressure cooker for the developers on the ground.
It usually plays out in three stages:

• Over-promising for Deals: Non-technical managers frequently agree to completely unrealistic client deadlines to secure a contract or hit a revenue target. Because they don’t understand technical debt or architectural complexity, they assume that throwing more “resources” (bodies) at the problem will make it go faster.

• Under-resourcing for Margins: To maximize profitability, that project is then staffed with a high ratio of inexperienced juniors and zero hands-on technical leads.

• The Squeeze: When the unrealistic deadline inevitably hits a roadblock, the managers do not step in to help architect a solution. Instead, they apply pressure.
Junior developers are forced into 12-to-14-hour shifts, weekend work, and relentless, anxiety-inducing status calls. When the project succeeds, the bonuses flow upward. When it stumbles, the blame flows downward.
The Psychological Toll on the Ground
Put yourself in the shoes of a 23-year-old fresher.
You are handed complex technical requirements without any senior technical mentorship. You are given impossible deadlines. You are micromanaged by a boss whose only metric is whether you logged 9 hours on the timesheet.
Is it any wonder that young developers are drowning in burnout and imposter syndrome? They feel like they are failing because they cannot meet standards that were functionally impossible to begin with.
Worse, the IT services sector often treats these young engineers as fungible commodities-literally calling them “billing resources.” If a young professional speaks up about burnout, the implicit threat is always lingering: There are thousands of desperate graduates waiting to take your place.
The Upstream Effect: The Campus Pressure Cooker
This anxiety doesn’t start on the first day of the job; it starts in college.
Students are acutely aware of the hyper-competitive, high-stress corporate environment awaiting them. The education system acts as a pipeline designed to force them into this exact IT pyramid.
Adding to the despair is economic reality. Entry-level salaries in Indian IT services have barely moved in the last 15 years-often still hovering around ₹3.5 to ₹4 LPA-while the cost of living and education has skyrocketed. The intense academic pressure is directly tied to the desperation of securing a job that will barely pay rent, let alone provide a healthy work-life balance.
HR “Band-Aids” Are Not the Cure
The most tragic part of this crisis is the corporate response. The treatment gap for mental health in India is massive, and speaking up about anxiety in the corporate IT world is still viewed as a “weakness” or a “performance issue.”
Instead of fixing the systemic rot, companies offer “Wellness Fridays,” pizza parties, or subscriptions to meditation apps.
Let me be clear: You cannot fix a mental health crisis with a yoga session when the root cause is a manager forcing a team to work 70-hour weeks to inflate a project’s gross margin.
The Way Forward: Bring Back the Senior IC
The shift away from deep, hands-on engineering leadership toward purely margin-driven management has broken the mentor-mentee relationship in Indian tech. It has transformed software development from a collaborative engineering discipline into a digital sweatshop.
We need to radically change how we value experience in this industry. We need to start rewarding senior Individual Contributors (ICs)-veterans who stay hands-on, who actually write code, who mentor juniors in the trenches, and who have the technical authority to look a Delivery Manager in the eye and say, “No, that deadline is technically impossible.”
Until the industry learns to value technical leadership as much as it values spreadsheet management, the heavy psychological cost will continue to be paid by the youngest, brightest, and most vulnerable people in the system.
It’s time we stop blaming the youth for their anxiety, and start looking at the system we built that is causing it.
